title: fix: use localhost for host.containers.internal in host network mode
body:
#### Checklist

- [x] Certify you wrote the patch or otherwise have the right to pass it on as an open-source patch by signing all commits. (`git commit -s`).
- [x] Referenced issues using `Fixes: #00000` in commit message (if applicable)
- [x] [Tests](https://github.com/containers/podman/tree/main/test#readme) have been added/updated (or no tests are needed)
- [ ] [Documentation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/README.md) has been updated (or no documentation changes are needed)
- [ ] All commits pass `make validatepr` (format/lint checks)
- [x] [Release note](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/release-notes.md) entered in the section below (or `None` if no user-facing changes)

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
With `--network=host`, `host.containers.internal` now resolves to `127.0.0.1` (localhost) instead of an external IP address that may change or not exist.
```

---

### Description

When using `--network=host`, the container shares the host's network namespace, so localhost (127.0.0.1) is always reachable. This change uses `127.0.0.1` for `host.containers.internal` instead of trying to find an external IP address.

This fixes issues where:
- The host IP address changes (e.g., DHCP lease renewal, roaming to a different network)
- The host has no non-loopback IP addresses configured

### How was it tested?

```bash
$ sudo ./bin/podman run --rm --network=host alpine cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1       localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1     localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
127.0.0.1       host.containers.internal host.docker.internal
```

Added a system test in `test/system/500-networking.bats` to verify the behavior.

Fixes #27823

comments.body: [NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.
[NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.

Thank you!

You've pretty much done exactly as I would have, this looks good to me.
(I checked rootless/rootfull and non-host in my environment just to make sure)

Should buildah ( https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/run_common.go#L171 - vendor/github.com/containers/buildah/run_common.go in the podman repo) get a similar update?
I don't think contacting the host is as common from buildah but it'd probably be more consistent to, although it's probably fine if the two are not in sync so I wouldn't block for it.

The only one failed seems to me a race condition, as it has nothing to do with the changes I made. 

In case, I try to commit a trigger to see if the second passes. 

As for buildah, I don't really know, if it has the same problem then it would be a solution to try to implement the same mechanism, I can't confirm as I don't use it.

I don't think buildah containers have much business starting early boot before network is configured (or be long lived and suffer through e.g. wifi roaming and IP change), so in my opinion it's much less important, but it's possible to run a container with `buildah from --network host docker.io/alpine` if one really wants to and have similar behavior (use `buildah run $container command` to check and `buildah rm $container` to cleanup)
(... actually as root it looks like buildah used my host's default interface ip address for host.containers.internal even in default bridged mode, so it's a bit weird... but can definitely be marked off as another problem, and I also don't use buildah so won't be annoying anyone with this)

Anyway, leaving this decision up to rhatdan or others, I just wanted to point out the other etchosts.New() caller in the code base -- thank you again :)

Do we care about ipv6 here?

Otherwise LGTM, given Matt kind of agreed with a way how to fix this in the github issue.
My theory was correct, there was a race condition.

> Practically speaking the argument the host address can chagne applies to all network modes so it is in no way special to host networking

The bridge address cannot change in practice (I guess it could if an admin goes out of their way to change it, but is that realistic?)

The problem that was reported to us in particular isn't so much network roaming and IP changing, but for a container starting up early at boot -- this is especially easy to hit with a LTE connection that can take up to a minute to bring up, while the container is started early; in that case `host.containers.internal` isn't set at all (as reported in the issue) unless passed with `--add-host` (which I did suggest as a first workaround in the issue)
In this case the name just isn't set, so using it never works even after an IP has been set.

> why no default to locahost/127.0.0.1 to begin with instead of relying on this custom name?

I more or less asked the same thing and the argument I was given was that they have multiple containers that are using or not using host networking, and don't want to change the software inside depending on the mode.
For our documentation (we provide an embedded platform with a web server reachable from containers), it's also helpful to have a stable name that'll allow reaching the host regardless of network mode, so I kind of agree with them here -- I'd very much like to support some kind of stable domain that "always works".

> So far the expectation of host.containers.internal has been it resolves to any non localhost host ip address so I don't really think special casing host here makes sense.

That's the first time I've read about this expectation, is that documented somewhere? For me, the expectation is that host.containers.internal allow reaching "the host", so e.g. if you have a web server on the host you can `curl host.containers.internal` and it'll just work.
I guess it's a change that could break users who run servers that listen to a specific interface and not to localhost, but in my experience that's fairly rare (the only kind of service I'm aware of that does this are dns servers, where you sometimes need to run a different server for local resolutions than for external ones)

If you feel strongly about the default behavior, perhaps a special value for `host_containers_internal_ip` could be set in containers.conf that'd allow using localhost if network=host but the current logic for bridged network?

> > Practically speaking the argument the host address can chagne applies to all network modes so it is in no way special to host networking
> 
> The bridge address cannot change in practice (I guess it could if an admin goes out of their way to change it, but is that realistic?)
> 

Bridge networking does indeed have a rather stable address by using the bridge address on the host. But that is just one of many different network modes, you can use macvlan, ipvaln where we cannot make any guarantees that the host namespace is reachable at all. As rootless we cannot use the bridge either as that is inside the custom netns not on the host so we need special logic there with pasta as well or like we used to on with slirp4netns just pick the first address so really it never was stable across a ton of different behaviours.

> The problem that was reported to us in particular isn't so much network roaming and IP changing, but for a container starting up early at boot -- this is especially easy to hit with a LTE connection that can take up to a minute to bring up, while the container is started early; in that case `host.containers.internal` isn't set at all (as reported in the issue) unless passed with `--add-host` (which I did suggest as a first workaround in the issue) In this case the name just isn't set, so using it never works even after an IP has been set.
> 
> > why no default to locahost/127.0.0.1 to begin with instead of relying on this custom name?
> 
> I more or less asked the same thing and the argument I was given was that they have multiple containers that are using or not using host networking, and don't want to change the software inside depending on the mode. For our documentation (we provide an embedded platform with a web server reachable from containers), it's also helpful to have a stable name that'll allow reaching the host regardless of network mode, so I kind of agree with them here -- I'd very much like to support some kind of stable domain that "always works".
> 

As written above we cannot offer something that "always works" today but I acknowledge that is not a argument to keep it worse if we can improve things.

> > So far the expectation of host.containers.internal has been it resolves to any non localhost host ip address so I don't really think special casing host here makes sense.
> 
> That's the first time I've read about this expectation, is that documented somewhere? For me, the expectation is that host.containers.internal allow reaching "the host", so e.g. if you have a web server on the host you can `curl host.containers.internal` and it'll just work. I guess it's a change that could break users who run servers that listen to a specific interface and not to localhost, but in my experience that's fairly rare (the only kind of service I'm aware of that does this are dns servers, where you sometimes need to run a different server for local resolutions than for external ones)
> 

Because allowing a container to reach the host netns localhost services breaks the expected isolation. Of course that argument is void for network=host as that is what was allowed anyway but still it means we then special case that to localhost where we would not previously.

> If you feel strongly about the default behavior, perhaps a special value for `host_containers_internal_ip` could be set in containers.conf that'd allow using localhost if network=host but the current logic for bridged network?

I rather not deal with more config options for something like this.
I guess if other maintainers such as @mheon agree with using 127.0.0.1 I am fine to accept this as well.

That said if we do move forward then the logic should be moved into GetHostContainersInternalIP() by passing a new `HostNamespace: bool` argument to that function and then we can pass that arg from podman and buildah like you mentioned already. All the logic to decide what ip to use should live in the same place.
@mheon could you take a look at it?
@mheon yes but yeah you are right the behavior is already different. I guess this will not break anyone so I am ok moving forward then with a patch on the container-libs repo to add the option there.

I added the changes to common. 

I hadn't noticed that the repository had been migrated. To avoid inconsistencies, I would recommend (if I may and if possible) archiving it.

In any case, I updated the lib to add the optional field, which should further strengthen the logic.

> I hadn't noticed that the repository had been migrated. To avoid inconsistencies, I would recommend (if I may and if possible) archiving it.

thanks for pointing that out.  I have brought this up with the team.
@Luap99 PTAL

please run
`go get go.podman.io/storage@main && go get go.podman.io/image/v5@main &&  go get go.podman.io/common@main && make vendor` and commit the result to update the vendored versions from cotnainer-libs.
Then update your code to use your new option instead. So commit 1 should be the vendor update and commit 2 your changes here with using the new option.

I know CI is still very red here, but as the Cockpit team keeps getting pinged: The [cockpit test](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/6e5fd530-2e33-4730-9569-c821ce651dac/work-podman-systemengvz976/plans/cockpit-podman/podman-system/execute/data/guest/default-0/test/browser/system-1/output.txt) failed because of `podman build` barfing with

> Error: building at STEP "RUN ls": encoding configuration for "buildah-oci-runtime": json: unsupported type: func(*url.URL) (*url.URL, error)

I suppose we'll look again here when your own CI has cleared up and c-podman's still fail? cockpit-podman's tests don't explicitly use `host.containers.internal`. Cockpit itself does, but that's not part of the reverse dependency checks here.

Thanks!
I then deleted all commits related to the ci

For now a PR like this is just not reviewable anymore.

I have noticed a few things that could contribute to race conditions. I will open issues about them, as suggested.
I have restored this PR to its original state.

I have verified on Fedora (local) that it compiles and installs correctly, so I can confirm that the problem is not in the code if it fails there.

Since, as I understand it, the issues are not my responsibility, I am marking the PR as ready for review.

The previous vendor issue that existed on Linux probably also exists on Darwin.
